message.content:
Adversarially verify this reverse-engineered architecture claim. Default to UNSUPPORTED if you cannot
confirm it from a PRIMARY source (paper, repo, official doc) or from the artifact text itself.

SYSTEM: openai-deepresearch (47.84) and gemini-2.5-pro-deepresearch (49.98, the RACE reference) on the GPT-5.5 board
HAS PAPER: true
ARCHITECTURE CLAIMED: TWO GENUINELY DIFFERENT ARCHITECTURES. Not variants of one design. Artifacts confirm the published descriptions.

== OpenAI DR: ONE MODEL, ONE ROLLOUT, NO PIPELINE ==
Deep Research System Card (OpenAI, 2025-02-25), sections 1-2, verbatim:
- "The deep research model is powered by an early version of OpenAI o3 that is optimized for web browsing."
- "The model learned the core browsing capabilities (searching, clicking, scrolling, interpreting files), how to use a python tool in a sandboxed setting ... and how to reason through and synthesize a large number of websites to find specific pieces of information or write comprehensive reports through reinforcement learning training on these browsing tasks."
- Training data ranged from "objective auto-gradable tasks with ground truth answers, to more open-ended tasks with accompanying rubrics for grading ... graded against the ground truth answers or rubrics using a chain-of-thought model as a grader."

So: NO planner module, NO separate writer, NO critic, NO outline artifact. Report-writing is an ACTION inside a single RL-trained agentic rollout. Report quality is baked into the WEIGHTS by rubric-graded RL (an LLM judge in the TRAINING loop, not at inference). Brief clarifying-question turn, then one continuous browse-reason loop, then the report is emitted from whatever is in context. All the machinery is at train time; nothing at inference time. This is why it is NOT copyable by us: the lever is RL, not scaffolding.

== Gemini 2.5 Pro DR: EXPLICIT PLAN -> ITERATE -> SELF-CRITIQUE PIPELINE ==
Google primary sources:
- ai.google.dev/gemini-api/docs/deep-research documents the loop literally as "Plan -> Search -> Read -> Iterate -> Output", explicitly contrasted against a normal model's "Generate -> Output". "The Gemini Deep Research Agent autonomously plans, executes, and synthesizes multi-step research tasks."
- gemini.google/overview/deep-research/ (THE load-bearing sentence): "Gemini critically evaluates the information, identifies key themes and inconsistencies, and structures the report in a logical and informative way, even performing multiple passes of self-critique to enhance clarity and detail."
- blog.google (Dec 2024 launch): "it creates a multi-step research plan for you to either revise or approve"; the loop is "searching, finding interesting pieces of information and then starting a new search based on what it has learned. It repeats this process multiple times"; and it leans on "the Gemini model's advanced reasoning capabilities and our 1M token context window".

So Gemini has, at INFERENCE time, three things OpenAI does not: (1) an explicit user-visible research PLAN produced before any browsing; (2) an explicit gap/discrepancy-driven iteration loop; (3) a REVISION LOOP over the drafted report ("multiple passes of self-critique"). And the writer sees the ENTIRE retrieved corpus at once in a 1M window.

== ARTIFACT FORENSICS (100 tasks each, our own JSONL) ==
Composition unit. Gemini outlines are DERIVED ANALYTIC FRAMES, not question mirrors. Task 51 (Japan elderly market) Gemini outline: 1. Executive Summary / 2. Evolving Landscape (2.1 Demographic Projections, 2.2 Deep Dive into Aging Trends, 2.3 Regional Dimensions) / 3. Economic Profile (3.1 Income, 3.2 Wealth, 3.3 Debt, 3.4 Critical Economic Factors) / 4. Current Consumption / 5. Future Trajectories / 6. Market Size Forecasts (6.1 Forecasting Approach ... 6.6 Discussion of Key Assumptions and Potential Variations) / 7. Strategic Implications and Conclusion (7.1 Quantifying the Longevity Economy Opportunity, 7.2 Navigating Challenges, 7.3 Recommendations, 7.4 Concluding Remarks). Note 6.1 and 6.6: it BUILDS a projection model and states its assumptions and their fragility. OpenAI on the same task: seven flat H2s that just mirror the question's own list (Food / Housing / Clothing / Transportation / Summary). No exec summary, no methodology, no implications section.

Structural spread (mean over 100):
- words: gemini 6727 (median 5705) vs openai 4743 (median 3192)
- H3-per-H2 (hierarchical decomposition): gemini 1.48 vs openai 0.52
- H3 count: gemini 11.4 vs openai 2.8
- tables: gemini 2.5/report, 95/100 reports have >=1 table; openai 0.5, only 35/100 have one
- bullet lines: gemini 187 vs openai 28 (but gemini bullets are ATOMIC: median 15 words; openai bullets are FAT: median 51, p90 202 -- openai's bullets ARE the content, gemini's are enumerated evidence hung off prose)
- unique URLs cited: gemini 113 vs openai 43
- headings containing implications/synthesis/strategic: gemini 29/100 vs openai 5/100
- headings containing limitation/caveat/uncertainty/risk: gemini 36/100 vs openai 11/100
- headings containing methodology/approach/assumptions: gemini 33/100 vs openai 18/100
- executive summary section: gemini 14/100, openai 0/100
- "taken together / collectively / synthesis / when combined" per 1k words: gemini 0.28 vs openai 0.08

Genre conditioning: Gemini scales DEPTH by genre (H3: how-to 22.8, compare 12.3, market 10.8, science 9.8; words: how-to 10950, market 9392, science 7470) but its TABLE use is genre-INVARIANT (a table appears in essentially every report regardless of genre). Tables are a house style, not a genre adaptation. OpenAI scales length similarly but never adds structure.

Citation mechanics differ completely. OpenAI: inline markdown links in the body, no bibliography (only 8/100 have a references section), 99/100 use [text](url). Gemini: bare superscript-style numerals glued to the text ("...robotics 3, a growing emphasis on health 20, and lifestyle shifts...11"), resolved by a global numbered "Works cited" list at the end (avg 113 unique sources).

Forensic test for one-pass vs. section-scoped writing (Gemini): I measured whether citation NUMBERS increase monotonically with first appearance in the body. Monotonicity = 0.63 (1.0 = numbering assigned by order of first use, 0.5 = random). It is only weakly linear => the numbered source list is a PRE-EXISTING enumeration of the retrieved corpus, and the writer cites INTO it from any point in the document. Combined with source reuse of 4.67 cite-tokens per distinct source, and 30% of sources appearing in MORE THAN ONE H2 section, this is GLOBAL-CONTEXT composition over a fully-retrieved corpus, not per-section RAG. A source is never "owned" by a section.
COMPOSITION METHOD CLAIMED: THE ANSWER TO THE QUESTION THAT MATTERS.

== GEMINI: retrieve-everything, then write ONE document against a THEME-derived outline, then self-critique ==
Sequence, as best reconstructed:
1. PLAN: an explicit multi-point research plan is emitted BEFORE any retrieval (user can edit it). The plan is over QUESTIONS/THEMES, not over sources.
2. ITERATE: browse -> read -> "identifies missing information and discrepancies to explore" -> browse again. Loop is gap-driven. It terminates when the model judges coverage sufficient. Result: ~113 distinct sources, all held in a 1M-token window.
3. OUTLINE FROM THEMES, NOT SOURCES: "identifies key themes and inconsistencies, and structures the report in a logical and informative way". The section tree is an ANALYTIC frame invented after reading (see task 51: "Forecasting Approach", "Discussion of Key Assumptions", "Navigating Challenges"). It routinely contains sections that exist NOWHERE in the user's question.
4. SINGLE GLOBAL-CONTEXT WRITE: one writer, whole corpus in context, whole outline in context. Evidence: citation numbers do not increase monotonically (0.63), sources are reused ~4.7x and cross 2+ sections 30% of the time. Section-scoped writers cannot do that.
5. REVISION: "multiple passes of self-critique to enhance clarity and detail" (Google's words). This is a real, documented critic loop at INFERENCE time.

== HOW SYNTHESIS IS ACTUALLY PRODUCED (the paragraph-level mechanism) ==
This is the finding that matters most for POLARIS. Gemini's insight is NOT produced by "writing unsourced opinion". Measure this:

  sentence-level UNCITED rate:   gemini 37.6%   openai 83.1%
  paragraphs where EVERY sentence is cited:  gemini 17.4%  openai 0.7%
  paragraphs where NO sentence is cited:     gemini 15.8%  openai 41.2%

Gemini is BOTH more densely grounded AND more insightful (insight 0.4992 vs 0.4669). OpenAI writes four times as much unsupported prose and scores LOWER on insight. So: uncited prose is NOT insight. This kills the naive "just let the model opine" fix.

What Gemini actually does is a CLAIM-EVIDENCE-WARRANT paragraph. Uncited-sentence rate by position within a paragraph: pos0 41.0%, pos1 35.0%, pos2 34.3%, pos3 34.5%, pos4(last) 38.8%. The paragraph OPENS with an uncited analytic claim, fills the middle with densely-cited evidence FROM MULTIPLE SOURCES, and CLOSES with an uncited inference. 25.8% of its multi-sentence paragraphs are exactly "cited body + uncited synthesis coda" (and those paragraphs are 28% likely to carry tension/trade-off/implication language vs 18% elsewhere).

Canonical example, verbatim, gemini task 63 (lithium niobate etching):
"The inherent characteristics of physical versus reactive etching methods present a fundamental challenge in simultaneously achieving both smooth and vertical sidewalls. Ar+ sputtering, being a physical process, tends to produce smoother surfaces due to its polishing effect [22] but lacks the chemical selectivity needed for high anisotropy, resulting in sloped sidewalls [22]. Conversely, reactive etching chemistries (e.g., fluorine-based) can offer better directionality and steeper profiles [22] but frequently suffer from the redeposition of non-volatile reaction byproducts like LiF, which inevitably leads to increased sidewall roughness.[1] This inherent conflict necessitates careful process selection and optimization, often involving trade-offs based on whether sidewall angle or smoothness is the more critical parameter."

Sentence 1: UNCITED analytic frame ("a fundamental challenge"). Sentences 2-3: CITED evidence from two different sources, deliberately juxtaposed. Sentence 4: UNCITED warrant that names the trade-off. Our span-verifier would delete sentence 1 and sentence 4 -- i.e. exactly the two sentences that carry the insight -- and leave a fact list.

Conflict handling is a first-class move, not an accident. Gemini task 53 reconciles rather than picks: it reports ADIA AUM at 708B [18] AND 1.11T [19], Norway GPFG at 1.34T [18] AND 1.74T [10], then adds the uncited meta-claim that "Different reporting organizations (SWF Institute, Global SWF, IFSWF) may use slightly different methodologies ... leading to discrepancies" and "These variations underscore the challenge of obtaining precise, real-time data for funds with lower transparency." It turns a citation CONFLICT into an INSIGHT about the domain. 44/100 Gemini reports contain explicit tension/conflict/discrepancy language vs 32/100 for OpenAI, and Gemini gives it a HEADING 42/100 times vs 14/100.

== OPENAI: no composition method at all, in the scaffolding sense ==
The report is the terminal emission of one RL rollout. It mirrors the question's structure, is flat (0.52 H3 per H2), has no executive summary in ANY of 100 reports, rarely tables, and is 83% uncited prose. Its instruction-following (49.29) is its best dimension precisely because it echoes the prompt's own shape. Its insight (46.69) is its worst. It is what our system would look like if we removed the span-verifier and let the model ramble: fluent, unsourced, structurally lazy.

== NEGATIVE RESULT WORTH FLAGGING (relevant to your recent length work) ==
38/100 OpenAI reports are under 1500 words. Their mean overall is 0.471 vs 0.483 for the other 62 -- a gap of only 1.2 points. Length is NOT what separates the two systems. Gemini's +2.14 overall and +3.23 insight over OpenAI is bought by ARGUMENT STRUCTURE (theme-derived outline, claim-evidence-warrant paragraphs, an explicit tensions/implications/limits section, a self-critique pass), not by word count. Chasing 8000 words will not move insight.
EVIDENCE CITED: PRIMARY / PUBLISHED
- OpenAI Deep Research System Card, 2025-02-25, https://cdn.openai.com/deep-research-system-card.pdf -- s1 "powered by an early version of OpenAI o3 that is optimized for web browsing"; s2 "Model data and training" ... "write comprehensive reports through reinforcement learning training on these browsing tasks" ... "graded against the ground truth answers or rubrics using a chain-of-thought model as a grader". (Fetched and text-extracted; quotes verbatim.)
- https://gemini.google/overview/deep-research/ -- "Gemini critically evaluates the information, identifies key themes and inconsistencies, and structures the report in a logical and informative way, even performing multiple passes of self-critique to enhance clarity and detail." (verbatim, fetched)
- https://ai.google.dev/gemini-api/docs/deep-research -- "Plan -> Search -> Read -> Iterate -> Output" vs a base model's "Generate -> Output"; "autonomously plans, executes, and synthesizes multi-step research tasks". (verbatim, fetched)
- https://blog.google/products/gemini/google-gemini-deep-research/ -- "it creates a multi-step research plan for you to either revise or approve"; iterative "searching, finding interesting pieces of information and then starting a new search based on what it's learned. It repeats this process multiple times"; "our 1M token context window". (verbatim, fetched)
- DeepResearch Bench, arXiv:2506.11763 (Du et al., 13 Jun 2025), https://arxiv.org/abs/2506.11763 -- source of the 100-task benchmark and the RACE framework. NOTE: I could NOT extract the RACE criterion definitions from the PDF (binary parse failed twice). The exact wording of what the Insight dimension rewards is therefore NOT verified by me -- do not quote it.

ARTIFACT (all measured by me, 100 tasks each)
- /home/polaris/polaris_project/drb_corpus/gpt55_board/openai-deepresearch.jsonl (100 reports)
- /home/polaris/polaris_project/drb_corpus/gpt55_board/gemini-2.5-pro-deepresearch.jsonl (100 reports)
- /home/polaris/polaris_project/drb_corpus/gpt55_board/scores/openai-deepresearch/race_result.txt -- Comprehensiveness 0.4805, Insight 0.4669, Instruction Following 0.4929
- /home/polaris/polaris_project/drb_corpus/gpt55_board/scores/gemini-2.5-pro-deepresearch/race_result.txt -- Comprehensiveness 0.5001, Insight 0.4992, Instruction Following 0.5022, Readability 0.4958, Overall 0.4998
- per-task scores: gpt55_board/scores/{system}/raw_results.jsonl (keys: id, prompt, comprehensiveness, insight, instruction_following, readability, overall_score)
- Verbatim synthesis paragraph quoted above: gemini-2.5-pro-deepresearch.jsonl, id=63. Conflict-reconciliation example: id=53. Derived-outline example: id=51 (compare against openai id=51, same prompt).
- Analysis scripts I wrote: /tmp/an.py through /tmp/an11.py (structure, rhetoric density, citation-per-sentence, citation-number monotonicity, cross-section source reuse, genre conditioning, uncited-position profile).

CAVEAT ON ONE NUMBER: my within-system feature-vs-insight correlations were all weak (|r| < 0.3) and I do not trust them; RACE is reference-relative and task-normalized, so within-system variance is mostly task difficulty. The cross-system contrast is where the signal is. Also, Gemini's citation markers are bare digits glued to text, which is easy to over-count; I re-ran with a corrected tokenizer and the numbers above are the corrected ones (my first pass wrongly reported 56% multi-cite sentences -- ignore that).

Check: does the cited source actually say this? Is it current (2026)? Is an "architecture" being inferred from
output text and then stated as fact? Inference from artifacts is LEGITIMATE but must be LABELLED as inference,
not presented as documented design. Flag any place that line is crossed.

- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

import json,re,statistics as st
def load(f): return {r['id']:r['article'] for r in map(json.loads,open(f))}
G=load('gemini-2.5-pro-deepresearch.jsonl'); O=load('openai-deepresearch.jsonl')
def stats(D,name):
    W=[];H2=[];H3=[];TB=[];BL=[];URL=[];ES=0
    for i,a in D.items():
        W.append(len(a.split()))
        h2=len(re.findall(r'^## (?!#)',a,re.M)); h3=len(re.findall(r'^### (?!#)',a,re.M))
        H2.append(h2);H3.append(h3)
        TB.append(len(re.findall(r'^\|[-: |]+\|\s*\$',a,re.M)))
        BL.append(len(re.findall(r'^\s*[-*+] ',a,re.M)))
        URL.append(len(set(re.findall(r'https?://[^\s)\]\">]+',a))))
        if re.search(r'^#+.*executive summary',a,re.M|re.I): ES+=1
    print(name)

    print('  H3 mean %.1f  H2 mean %.1f  H3/H2 %.2f'%(st.mean(H3),st.mean(H2),sum(H3)/sum(H2)))
    print('  tables mean %.2f  >=1 table: %d/100'%(st.mean(TB),sum(1 for t in TB if t>=1)))
    print('  bullets mean %.0f'%st.mean(BL))
    print('  unique URLs mean %.0f'%st.mean(URL))
    print('  exec summary heading: %d/100'%ES)
stats(G,'GEMINI'); stats(O,'OPENAI')

content.content:
# Deep Research Feature Overview

## Multi-Step Research Plan
"After you enter your question, it creates a multi-step research plan for you to either revise or approve."

## Iterative Search Process
"It repeats this process multiple times" after "starting a new search based on what it's learned."

## Context Window Capability
Google has integrated "our 1M token context window" to enable Deep Research to generate comprehensive reports with detailed insights.

These three components work together to allow Gemini's Deep Research to autonomously investigate topics while keeping users in control of the research direction.

# Gemini Deep Research Agent: Key Differences

**Agent Process Loop:**
The documentation contrasts the Deep Research Agent's approach with standard models, stating: "Plan -> Search -> Read -> Iterate -> Output" versus the standard model's "Generate -> Output."

**Autonomous Planning:**
The agent "autonomously plans, executes, and synthesizes multi-step research tasks" according to the documentation's opening description.

**Why This Matters:**
Unlike conventional models that generate responses immediately, Deep Research operates as an "analyst-in-a-box," conducting iterative web searches and analysis over several minutes before synthesizing findings into detailed reports.

input.reasoning:
VERIFIED BY RE-EXECUTION (I recomputed everything from the JSONL, not trusting the numbers).

== WHAT IS SOLID ==
1. All four primary-source quotes are VERBATIM. I extracted the system card PDF locally (pypdf, 36pp) after WebFetch's parse failed — same failure the author admitted. s1 "powered by an early version of OpenAI o3 that is optimized for web browsing" and the whole s2 RL/rubric/"chain-of-thought model as a grader" passage are exact. gemini.google self-critique sentence: exact. ai.google.dev "Plan -> Search -> Read -> Iterate -> Output" + "autonomously plans": exact. blog.google plan/iterate/1M: exact. The headline "two genuinely different architectures" is well-supported.
2. Scores exact. Structure stats reproduce almost perfectly: words 6727/5705 vs 4743/3192 (exact), H3 11.4 vs 2.8 (exact), tables 2.5/0.5 (exact), URLs 113 vs 43 (exact), bullets 187 vs 28 (exact once numbered lists counted), exec-summary 0/100 for OpenAI (exact).
3. Artifacts real: task 51 derived outline (incl. "6.1 Forecasting Approach"), task 53 ADIA/GPFG conflict, task 63 quote all present.
4. The length negative result reproduces EXACTLY: 38 OpenAI reports <1500w, 0.4714 vs 0.4826, gap 1.12pts. "Chasing 8000 words won't move insight" stands.
5. The killer fact stands: Gemini 38.8% uncited sentences vs OpenAI 79.7%, yet Gemini scores higher on insight. Uncited prose is NOT insight. Robust.

== WHAT BREAKS ==
A. THE MONOTONICITY INFERENCE IS INVERTED — the most serious defect. The claim reads monotonicity 0.63 as "only weakly linear => the numbered source list is a PRE-EXISTING enumeration of the retrieved corpus, and the writer cites INTO it from any point." I ran that exact hypothesis as a permutation null. A pre-existing enumeration (numbering independent of text order) predicts Spearman 0.02 / frac-increasing 0.51. OBSERVED: Spearman 0.59 (median 0.62), with 59/72 reports above 0.4. The author's own frac-increasing metric (I reproduce it: 0.60) is an insensitive adjacent-pairs statistic; the rank correlation shows citation numbers ARE substantially assigned in order of first use, which REFUTES the pre-existing-enumeration story. The conclusion (single global-context write) probably survives — sequential single-pass writing with a bibliography numbered in first-use order predicts exactly what I see — but it survives DESPITE the stated evidence, not because of it. The real support is cross-section reuse (I confirm 29% of sources cross 2+ H2 sections, 4.14 cite-tokens/source), which the claim treats as secondary.

B. THE HEADLINE PARAGRAPH MECHANISM HALF-FAILS. "Claim-evidence-warrant" is called "the finding that matters most for POLARIS." The uncited analytic OPENING is real and strong (pos0 66.8% uncited in my run — even more pronounced than claimed). But the uncited synthesis CODA does not reproduce: in my measurement the LAST sentence is the MOST-cited position (39.1% uncited, the minimum), not an uncited inference. The claim's profile is a U-shape (41.0/35.0/34.3/34.5/38.8); mine is open-uncited-then-increasingly-cited. The "25.8% cited-body+uncited-coda" figure comes out 12.3%, and its validation stat — coda paragraphs 28% vs 18% likely to carry tension language — comes out 21% vs 20%, i.e. NO effect. The task-63 example is a real n=1 anecdote, not a demonstrated pattern.

C. ARCHITECTURE FROM SILENCE, STATED AS FACT. "NO planner module, NO separate writer, NO critic, NO outline artifact" is inferred from a SAFETY system card that never discusses inference-time architecture. I grepped the full card: 0 hits for planner/subagent/multi-agent/self-critique (the 10 "critic" hits are all the word "Critical" in risk tables). Absence in a safety document is weak evidence of architectural absence — the card never denies a pipeline. This is a reasonable inference but it is presented as documented design; that line IS crossed. Relatedly, the "brief clarifying-question turn" is nowhere in the card (0 hits for clarify/clarifying) — that's product behavior, unsourced.

D. "Works cited" is a fabricated label. The literal string appears in 0/100 Gemini reports; there is no References/Bibliography/Sources heading in any of them. The numbered bibliography is real (98/100) but is an UNHEADED trailing numbered list.

E. GEMINI'S 0.50s ARE BY CONSTRUCTION, AND IT IS 7th OF 10. Gemini scores 0.5001/0.4992/0.5022/0.4958/0.4998 — dead 0.50 on every dimension because it IS the RACE reference scoring against itself. The author flags it's the reference but still uses its scores as evidence its architecture is superior. Six systems on this very board BEAT it (cellcog-max 55.78, WhaleCloud 54.78, bodhi 54.07, lunon 53.51, dalpha 53.10, sourcery 51.17). Holding up the 7th-place system as the architecture to copy is a material omission for the practical conclusion.

F. CURRENCY. Sources are Dec 2024 / Feb 2025; the Gemini artifacts are date-stamped "访问时间为 四月 28, 2025" (accessed 28 Apr 2025) — they are DeepResearch Bench's released outputs, not freshly generated. Nothing here is 2026. Fine as a description of the systems AS BENCHMARKED, but the claim writes in the present tense and never dates the artifacts.

G. Minor numeric drift: H3-per-H2 "gemini 1.48" reproduces under no convention I tried (aggregate 2.19, per-report 2.24; OpenAI 0.52 is right) — direction is understated, not overstated. "Executive summary 14/100" → 8 as a heading, 12 as any mention. The task-63 quote, presented as "verbatim," was silently reformatted (bare digits 22/1 → "[22]"/"[1]") and one clause dropped — ironic given the claim itself documents that Gemini uses bare digits.

== NET ==
The architectural dichotomy, the primary sourcing, the structural forensics, the grounding-vs-insight inversion, and the length negative result all hold up. But two of the three inferences the author bills as most decision-relevant — the "pre-existing enumeration / global-context write" citation forensic and the "uncited synthesis coda" paragraph mechanism — are contradicted or unreproduced by my re-analysis, and the OpenAI "no pipeline" architecture is inference-from-silence dressed as documented design. PARTIAL, not CONFIRMED.
